title: Hodge Theory and Representations of Real Groups
speaker: Dougal Davis
abstract: In a seminal paper from 2011, Wilfried Schmid and Kari Vilonen made a bold proposal that apparently intractable questions in the representation theory of real reductive groups could be resolved with the help of natural Hodge structures coming from geometry. In this talk, I will survey to what extent this program has been realised, explaining places in representation theory where Hodge structures can indeed be seen to play an important role (such as Vogan’s theory of lowest K-types, unitarity questions and the Orbit Method) and some of the techniques that go into making this a working theory. This is joint work with Kari Vilonen and with Lucas Mason-Brown.

proof_stages.label: Representation-theoretic problems and Hodge-theoretic strategy
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up real reductive Lie groups and motivating problems such as classifying irreducible unitary representations, understanding orbit-method structure, and describing representation categories, then states the Schmid-Vilonen idea that a Hodge theory for real-group representations should address these problems.

proof_stages.label: Harish-Chandra modules as algebraic models
proof_stages.summary: Topological representations of the real group are replaced by algebraic Harish-Chandra, or (g,K), modules, retaining the infinitesimal Lie-algebra action and the maximal compact subgroup action while discarding inessential choices of function space.

proof_stages.label: Axioms for mixed Hodge (g,K)-modules
proof_stages.summary: The desired category of mixed Hodge (g,K)-modules is specified by tensoring with complex mixed Hodge structures, pure objects and weights, an exact faithful forgetful functor to filtered finite-length (g,K)-modules, and essentially unique Hodge lifts of irreducibles with real infinitesimal character.

proof_stages.label: Polarizations and signature predictions
proof_stages.summary: Pure objects are expected to carry polarizations corresponding to invariant Hermitian forms with Hodge-Riemann sign rules, and the resulting theory is asserted to exist at least strongly enough to predict and prove signature information relevant to unitarity.

proof_stages.label: Flag-variety localization construction
proof_stages.summary: The category is constructed geometrically by passing to the complex group, its flag variety, differential operators on that flag variety, and the Beilinson-Bernstein equivalence, including the K-equivariant version that matches (g,K)-modules.

proof_stages.label: Mixed Hodge module input
proof_stages.summary: Saito-style mixed Hodge modules, in a complex-coefficient version, supply the needed geometric objects: regular holonomic D-modules with weight, Hodge, and conjugate Hodge filtrations satisfying the Hodge-module conditions, with later discussion of the analytic nature of such theories.

proof_stages.label: Filtered exactness via Hodge-filtration acyclicity
proof_stages.summary: To make the geometric construction satisfy the desired forgetful-functor property, one proves that Beilinson-Bernstein acyclicity and global generation hold not only for the D-module but for each Hodge-filtration piece, yielding strict exactness after global sections.

proof_stages.label: Applications to lowest pieces, unitarity, and categories
proof_stages.summary: The Hodge filtration and associated graded are applied to lowest-K-type phenomena, bounds and unitarity results for special unipotent representations, orbit-method calculations, and prospective categorical descriptions via coherent sheaves and intertwining functors.

examples.name: Finite-dimensional representations as a comparison case
examples.note: The audience asks about finite-dimensional representations, and the answer explains that they essentially come from complexified Lie-algebra/algebraic-group representations and hence do not see the real form, motivating the infinite-dimensional focus.
examples.engagement_reason: Finite-dimensional representations are used only as a comparison case, with facts stated about algebraic/Lie-algebra origins and trivial Hodge-filtration behavior but no concrete calculation or construction.

passes.reason: Finite-dimensional representations form a standard algebraic comparison family, not a sporadic or limiting object.

examples.name: The SL2(R) representation on L2(R union infinity)
examples.note: The running concrete representation has SL2(R) acting on the projective line/circle; its Harish-Chandra module is described by finite Fourier polynomials and later by the D-module O_{P1}(*{0,infinity}) with its pole-order Hodge filtration.
examples.engagement_reason: The SL2(R) action on L2(R∪∞) is carried through to its Harish-Chandra model, corresponding D-module on P1 with poles at 0 and ∞, explicit Hodge filtration, cohomology check, K-type behavior, and weight filtration.

passes.reason: The L2 action of SL2(R) on the projective line is a standard principal-series-type infinite-dimensional representation used as a running example.

examples.name: Principal series parameter versus K-types
examples.note: A principal-series family is used to illustrate that the Hodge filtration is not determined only by K-types: as the parameter grows, the lowest Hodge piece becomes larger and absorbs more K-types.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes how a principal-series parameter affects the Hodge filtration and K-types, noting the small/large parameter behavior and, in the SL2 case, one-dimensional K-types with F0 three-dimensional and later pieces adding K-types one at a time.

passes.reason: Varying principal-series parameters with the same K-type pattern is used to show the Hodge filtration is not determined just by K-types.
The principal-series family is used to show that the Hodge filtration is not determined just by K-types, since changing the parameter changes it.
